topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes hung heavy in the studio, thick and suffocating. Elias leaned back on his stool, the canvas before him mocking his inertia. His hand, usually a flurry of confident strokes, felt heavy, the charcoal dragging across the paper with agonizing slowness. He blinked several times, the light in the room seeming too bright, too demanding. He shifted, a deep, bone-weary sigh escaping his lips, and let his gaze drift over the completed piece—a vibrant, chaotic landscape he’d labored over for weeks. Then, his eyes snagged on a detail near the corner, something unnervingly precise, a line that didn't belong to his own execution. A prickle of coldness traced its way down his spine. He squinted, leaning closer, the movement stiff. It was a tattoo. A different style, stark and undeniable, woven into the very fabric of his vision.

[story 2]

Maya stared at the drying oil paint, the colors smeared across her palette looking dull and lifeless under the weak afternoon sun filtering through the dusty window. Every muscle in her neck felt tight, protesting the effort of simply existing. She picked up a brush, the weight of it immense, and found her movements jerky, hesitant. As she stepped back to assess the composition, her peripheral vision caught it—a dark, intricate pattern overlaid on the central figure she’d painstakingly rendered. It wasn't a mistake; it was deliberate. A permanent mark, etched into the very essence of her creation. A strange tremor ran through her fingers, making the brush tremble almost uncontrollably.

[story 3]

Arthur shuffled through the cramped workshop, the floorboards seeming to resist his weight. His eyelids felt weighted with lead, making it a monumental task just to focus on the easel. He approached his latest portrait, intending only a cursory glance, but the sight arrested him. There, beneath the carefully blended shadows of the subject’s cloak, was a tattoo—sharp, almost aggressive in its definition, clearly applied by another hand. Arthur stopped dead, his breath catching in his throat. He felt a profound sense of dislocation, as if a vital piece of his own reality had been subtly, yet irrevocably, altered by an unseen presence. He stood there, rooted, unable to move or even process the sudden intrusion.
